name: Oral / subcutaneous vs intravenous opioids
intervention: Opioid standard favoring oral and subcutaneous routes
summary: Inpatient stewardship programs that prioritize oral and subcutaneous opioids over intravenous routes cut IV opioid exposure by roughly 70–85% without worsening pain, and observational data link intravenous administration to substantially more adverse events and downstream opioid use.
description: Intravenous opioids act faster and carry more euphoric potential than oral or subcutaneous routes, and observational data link inpatient IV exposure to more adverse events and later opioid use. Several hospitals adopted standards favoring oral, then subcutaneous, routes. The evidence is a mix of pre/post quality-improvement projects and observational cohorts.
interpretation: Read these as exposure comparisons, not clinical-event trials. The pre/post programs (Ackerman, Amell, Dalal) cut IV opioid use 70–85% without worsening pain. NNT mostly does not apply—the outcomes are rates and adjusted odds. The adverse-event and downstream-opioid rows are observational, inverted to favor the oral/SC route. No randomized trial shows the strategy improves hard clinical outcomes.
methodologyNotes: Rate ratios and inverted odds ratios; confidence intervals are derived from reported means or inverted from published IV-referenced estimates. Pre/post and observational designs limit causal inference.

outcomes.label: IV opioid exposure
outcomes.description: Intravenous opioid administration (doses per patient-day) after a route-stewardship intervention.

outcomes.label: Total opioid exposure (MME)
outcomes.description: Total opioid exposure in morphine-milligram-equivalents per patient-day.

outcomes.label: Opioid adverse events (oral/SC vs IV)
outcomes.description: Acute opioid-related adverse events by route of administration.

outcomes.label: Downstream opioid exposure
outcomes.description: Subsequent/post-discharge opioid exposure associated with inpatient IV opioids.

studies.author: Ackerman
studies.citation: Ackerman AL, O'Connor PG, Doyle DL, et al. Association of an Opioid Standard of Practice Intervention With Intravenous Opioid Exposure in Hospitalized Patients. JAMA Intern Med. 2018;178(5):759-763.
studies.population: Urban academic medical unit (287 vs 127 patients)
studies.notes: Original urban study. IV opioid doses 0.39 → 0.06 per patient-day (84% reduction, p<0.001). No CI was published; rate-ratio CI computed by treating doses as Poisson counts over patient-days (4,500 pre, 2,459 post). Pain did not worsen and improved by day 4-5.

studies.author: Amell
studies.citation: Amell F, Sikora R, Beaudry JR, et al. Better opioid responsibility: implementing novel guidance as an opioid standard of care in academic and rural settings. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2026.
studies.population: Two rural hospitals (347 vs 263 patients; 15,749 patient-days)
studies.notes: Rural replication of the Ackerman standard. IV opioid doses 0.158 → 0.043 per patient-day (73% reduction, p<0.001). Rate-ratio CI from the reported arm means and their 95% CIs. Pain scores did not increase (balancing measure).

studies.author: Dalal
studies.citation: Dalal RS, et al. A Multimodal Intervention Using Nonopioid Analgesics Is Associated With Reduced Intravenous Opioid Exposure Among Hospitalized Patients With Inflammatory Bowel Diseases. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2021.
studies.notes: Proportion of patients receiving ANY IV opioid: 43.6% → 30.8% (p=0.03). A different exposure metric than the dose-rate studies, so shown for context and excluded from the pooled IV-exposure estimate. Pain unchanged (3.9 → 3.7); LOS and 30-day readmission also fell.

studies.author: Amell
studies.citation: Amell F, Sikora R, Beaudry JR, et al. Better opioid responsibility: implementing novel guidance as an opioid standard of care in academic and rural settings. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2026.
studies.notes: Total opioid MME 14.46 → 7.65 per patient-day (47% reduction, p<0.001). Rate-ratio CI from the reported arm means and their 95% CIs.

studies.author: Ackerman
studies.citation: Ackerman AL, O'Connor PG, Doyle DL, et al. Association of an Opioid Standard of Practice Intervention With Intravenous Opioid Exposure in Hospitalized Patients. JAMA Intern Med. 2018;178(5):759-763.
studies.notes: Overall opioid MME 9.11 → 6.30 per patient-day (31% reduction). CI approximated from the reported means and SDs (n=patients); patient-day clustering not modeled, so the true interval is wider.

studies.author: Daoust
studies.citation: Daoust R, Paquet J, Lavigne G, et al. Impact of age, sex and route of administration on adverse events after opioid treatment in the emergency department: a retrospective study. Pain Res Manag. 2015;20(1):23-28.
studies.notes: Global adverse events, oral vs IV (inverted from the published adjusted IV-vs-oral OR 5.18, 4.13-6.49). Major adverse events oral vs IV ≈ 0.16 (inverted from 6.10). Subcutaneous is intermediate (SC-vs-oral OR 2.23).

studies.author: Dalal
studies.citation: Dalal RS, Palchaudhuri S, Snider CK, et al. Exposure to Intravenous Opioids Is Associated With Future Exposure to Opioids in Hospitalized Patients With Inflammatory Bowel Diseases. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2020;18(10):2269-2278.
studies.notes: Post-discharge opioid prescription, no-IV vs IV (inverted from the published adjusted IV-vs-no-IV OR 3.3, 1.7-6.4). In the IBD-flare subgroup the association was stronger (inverted OR ≈ 0.19).
